data.title: Wisconsin DOJ sues Kalshi, Robinhood, Polymarket, Coinbase, Crypto.com over sports betting
data.description:
The Wisconsin Department of Justice has filed lawsuits against Kalshi (KALSHI), Robinhood (HOOD), Polymarket (POLYMARKET), Coinbase (COIN), and Crypto.com over illegal sports betting.

The state department alleges that the companies flouted the laws by disguising sports betting on their online platforms as "event contracts".

Furthermore, the complaint alleges that the accused firms collect a fee for every bet made, thereby generating revenue by violating the state's gambling laws.

"Thinly disguising unlawful conduct doesn't make it lawful," said AG Kaul. "These companies' alleged facilitation of sports betting in Wisconsin should be shut down."

The lawsuits, requiring preliminary and permanent injunctions, were filed in Dane County on Thursday.

Wisconsin is the latest state to take action against prediction markets over sports-related event contracts.

Currently, the U.S. prediction market is sitting in a rapidly evolving but still unsettled regulatory environment. The CFTC has moved toward a formal framework, including a March 12 advance notice of proposed rulemaking and a staff advisory, while also withdrawing an earlier proposal that could have tightened limits on sports- and politics-related contracts.

At the same time, state regulators and litigants are aggressively challenging the industry, especially where contracts overlap with gambling or sports betting. The result today is a patchwork of federal oversight, active rulemaking, and ongoing state-level legal pressure.

Earlier this month, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission filed a lawsuit against three states—Arizona, Connecticut, and Illinois—over unlawful efforts to regulate prediction markets.

data.title: Amerant projects 2Q 2026 deposits of $8B and targets a ~60% efficiency ratio as Venezuela-driven inflows build
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amerant Bancorp (AMTB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “I’m proud of the continued progress we have made on the 3 priorities we outlined last quarter, stabilizing the business, optimizing our credit portfolio and growing sustainably.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Carlos Iafigliola)

  • “We exited and transferred to held for sale another group of loans that we no longer consider core to our business,” and the company “fundamentally shifted underwriting, prioritizing borrowers with proven stable operating history over projection-based lending” while “moving away from out-of-market collateral projects, except selectively for existing clients in core markets.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • “Our net income for Q1 was in line with our guidance,” and management said it “significantly reduced noninterest expenses quarter-over-quarter, supported by better-than-expected cost savings,” adding, “our expense management efforts represents approximately $30 million in cost savings for 2026.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • “This quarter, we delivered, recording $188 million of total deposit growth in Q1, from which $95 million came from Venezuela,” and management described these as “quite attractive due to their stability, overall cost of funds and beta in rates-up cycle.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • “Going forward, we will be discussing results without breaking down core versus noncore metrics in our financials,” adding, “we would like to be more selective with adjustments with the goal of providing a clearer and more straightforward view of our quarterly performance.” (Senior EVP & CFO Sharymar Yepez)

  • “We redesigned our annual review format to drive deeper risk identification and recalibrated the review threshold from total credit exposures of $5 million to $3 million,” and “expect to review all exposures over $1 million through our standardized review,” alongside “quarterly top 20 reviews across CRE, C&I and Private Banking segments.” (Chief Credit Officer & Senior EVP Lee Cragg)

Outlook

  • “For 2Q '26, we project loan balances to reach approximately $7 billion,” and “for the full year 2026, we expect annualized loan growth of approximately 7%,” with growth governed by “2 deliberate and nonnegotiable priorities,” including continuing to “exit certain credits” and pursuing growth “consistent with our risk appetite.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • “We expect deposits to reach $8 billion by 2Q '26 and cumulative deposit growth between 8% to 10% for 2026,” and said that confidence is “supported by emerging opportunities in Venezuela.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • “We expect net interest margin to be in the 3.4% to 3.5% range in 2Q '26, stabilizing around 3.4% towards year-end,” and projected “approximately $68 million to $69 million in expenses for 2Q '26” with “quarterly expenses stabilizing around $68 million by the second part of the year” as the bank works “towards our target efficiency ratio of approximately 60%.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Compared with Q4 2025 guidance language, management moved from projecting 1Q 2026 net interest margin of “3.65% to 3.70%” and expenses “$70 million to $71 million in the first half of 2026” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, Q4 call) to a lower 2Q 2026 NIM range and a tighter expense run-rate centered on ~$68 million. (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, Q1 call)

Financial Results

  • “Diluted income per share for the first quarter was $0.44 compared to $0.07 in the fourth quarter,” while “net interest income was $80.3 million, down $9.9 million from $90.2 million in the fourth quarter,” and “the contraction of our financial margin” was “to 3.55% from 3.78%.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • “Noninterest expense was $66.9 million, down by $39.9 million or 37.3% from $106.8 million,” and management cited cost actions including “$3.3 million savings in vendor contract renegotiations,” partially offset by “$1.7 million in an impairment on investment carried at cost and $1.8 million in net losses on loans held for sale.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Capital actions continued: “CET1 remains strong at 11.84%,” and during Q1 the bank “repurchased 859,493 shares at a weighted average price of $21.77” and paid a “$0.09 per share” dividend, with the board approving another “$0.09 per share payable on May 29.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Credit metrics and exits were a central focus: “Nonperforming loans were up $4.7 million or 2.7% for a total of $176.1 million,” and the company reported “loan payoffs totaling $59.5 million and loans sold totaling $65.7 million during the period.” (Chief Credit Officer & Senior EVP Cragg)

Q&A

  • Evan Yee, Raymond James: asked why expenses ran better than expected and how durable savings are; Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola said the bank “accelerated some of the contract renegotiation,” and Senior EVP & CFO Yepez said the goal is “sustainability quarter-over-quarter,” while also expecting investments in “people and technology” as the bank plans to “cross the $10 billion threshold.”

  • Evan Yee, Raymond James: asked about buyback appetite; Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola said “the leftover of the buyback right now is $21 million” and management is “planning to complete the buyback through Q2.”

  • Nicholas Lorenzoni, Stephens: asked if the mid-2Q CRE exit “wrap[s] up the heavy lifting on derisking”; Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola said the next phase is “a risk calibration exercise,” with “another exit up coming weeks,” leaving “$130 million in available for sale,” and said “the plan is to continue to execute” to build “a portfolio that is more granular.”

  • Wood Lay, KBW: questioned why NIM came in below guidance and whether safer production is lower yielding; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez cited “a different asset mix,” “timing of the funding of the loan growth,” and that new production aligned with risk appetite is expected to come “with an overall lower yield,” while noting “I didn’t see anything significant this quarter” on nonaccrual impacts.

  • Wood Lay, KBW: asked how the bank is scaling Venezuela and what deposit costs look like; Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola said the bank is “looking to increase the staff,” described “a path towards reducing the number of sanctions,” and said international deposit costs are “around 1.30% actually even a little bit lower 1.15%,” while “the incremental deposits that we’re getting are actually sub 1%.”

  • Wood Lay, KBW: asked about charge-off expectations amid geopolitical/inflation concerns; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez said, “we have no direct exposure to exploration or extraction on the oil piece,” and guided to charge-offs of “around 30 to 35 basis points.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on execution details (expenses durability, NIM under-guidance, remaining derisking) with questions like “does that effectively wrap up the heavy lifting on derisking?” (Nicholas Lorenzoni, Stephens) and “It came below the guide you all had given for the quarter.” (Wood Lay, KBW)

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and process-driven in prepared remarks (e.g., “strong execution and tangible progress” and “remain confident”), and more explanatory in Q&A with bridges and caveats such as “we’re carefully assessing right now” on margin sensitivity to international deposits. (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • Versus Q4 2025, management used more definitive execution language on costs and Venezuela-related deposit inflows in Q1 2026, while Q4 framed Venezuela as early-stage: “It’s still too soon to understand the impact.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola, Q4 call)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized realized Venezuela-linked deposit inflows and cross-sell into wealth, while Q4 2025 positioned Venezuela as an “opportunity” tied to reopening dynamics; Q1 quantified that “$95 million came from Venezuela” out of “$188 million of total deposit growth.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • Expense narrative shifted from restructuring-driven noise in Q4 (including “contract termination costs” and “staff separation costs”) to a stabilized run-rate in Q1: “the run-up rate seems to be closer to the $68 million for the entire year quarter-over-quarter.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • Credit work progressed from broad review coverage in Q4 to tighter monitoring and ongoing exits in Q1, including new portfolio-management staffing and lower review thresholds, alongside continued loan sales/payoffs and projections for additional CRE exits. (Chief Credit Officer & Senior EVP Cragg)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged that near-term profitability can be pressured by credit exits and NPLs: “While this may have a short-term impact, it improves the long-term sustainability of our business.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Funding and margin risk remained tied to rate and deposit repricing timing: “There’s still uncertainty as to the timing of the repricing of those deposits,” which management said “will impact the guidance to the NIM.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Credit resolution execution risk persisted alongside downgrades: NPLs rose to “$176.1 million,” and management described continued work on “restructures or obtaining additive credit enhancements where possible.” (Chief Credit Officer & Senior EVP Cragg)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of executing its stabilization plan through lower expenses, continued credit exits and upgraded monitoring, and a notable step-up in international deposits tied to Venezuela. For Q2 and full-year 2026, the company guided to loan growth and deposit growth targets while projecting net interest margin to settle in the mid-3% range and quarterly expenses to stabilize near $68 million as it works toward an approximately 60% efficiency ratio, alongside ongoing share repurchases and dividends.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Zoom should put itself up for sale if it fails to drive up shareholder value: Spruce Point
data.description:
Spruce Point Management suggests the digital communication platform Zoom (ZM) should place itself on the auction block if it fails to drive up meaningful shareholder value within the next year.

However, the firm believes the company has upside potential, and several remedies can be applied before taking such a drastic measure.

"After conducting a forensic review of Zoom Communications, Inc., we see material upside potential in the Company's shares," Spruce Point said. "Zoom is the leader in video communications and was a COVID-19 pandemic darling. However, a growth slowdown, persistent skepticism regarding its market opportunity and competitive position, and the current SaaS-pocalypse have, we believe unfairly, driven Zoom's valuation to egregiously low levels, particularly considering the Company’s estimated $1.2 billion stake in AI leader Anthropic (ANTHRO)."

To be fair, Zoom shares have not suffered the same fate as most of the software sector has recently. It is up 6% year to date, while the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) has declined 20% over that time frame.

To accelerate shareholder value further, Spruce Point suggested several recommendations for the company. These include improving capital allocation, reallocating and reducing operating expenses, restructuring and refocusing the international business, and improving marketing. Additional remedies recommended are avoiding M&A mistakes, consolidating insider selling, collapsing its dual-class share structure, and lobbying for inclusion in the S&P 500 (SP500).

If all these fail, Spruce Point said the company should put itself up for sale.

"We believe Zoom should pursue a sale transaction if management cannot drive shareholder value creation within a year," Spruce Point added. "We view Zoom as a highly attractive acquisition target and believe there is a broad universe of potential strategic buyers with massive financial wherewithal. Our analysis of the largest software buyouts of the past five years suggests a 33% premium from private equity could set an M&A valuation floor."

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq rise, Dow lags after Israel-Lebanon ceasefire extension
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.7%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.5%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.1%.

Intel (INTC) shares jumped 22.6% after its Q1 results delivered a beat, and now its second-quarter revenue outlook is expected to surpass what analysts anticipated. Other semiconductors also rose, including Nvidia (NVDA), TSMC (TSM), AMD (AMD), Arm Holdings (ARM), and Qualcomm (QCOM).

Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points lower at 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting ambassadors of both countries in Washington. 

"The meeting went very well! The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah. The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by THREE WEEKS," he said via Truth Social.

Trump also ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Moreover, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy laid more mines in the Strait of Hormuz this week, according to a report by Axios.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly lower at $93, while Brent (CO1:COM) was last around $104 per barrel.

On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April—to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March—according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

data.title: Kinsale signals focus on sustaining "low 20s" ROE as commercial property competition persists
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kinsale Capital Group (KNSL) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "In the first quarter 2026, Kinsale's diluted operating earnings per share increased by 37.7% over the first quarter of 2025, generating an annualized operating return on equity of 24%." (Chairman of the Board, CEO & President Michael Kehoe)

  • "Gross written premium was down 0.5%, but net written premium grew by 5.6% for the quarter" and "Kinsale's combined ratio was 77.4%." (CEO Kehoe)

  • "Much of the headwind to our growth emanates from our large commercial property division" with "an abundance of competition and falling rates," while "excluding the Commercial Property division, Kinsale's growth in gross written premium was 6% for the first quarter." (CEO Kehoe)

  • "In the first quarter, new business submissions were up 6%. New business quotes were up 8% and new business bind orders were up 9%." (CEO Kehoe)

  • "Our average policy premium for the quarter... was $12,200 per policy, down from $14,200" in Q1 2025, which management tied to a pivot toward smaller transactions where "margins continue to be robust." (CEO Kehoe)

  • "Net income and net operating earnings" increased "by 26.1% and 36.3%, respectively, quarter-over-quarter," and "diluted earnings per share continues to improve and was $5.11 per share" in Q1. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bryan Petrucelli)

  • "The 77.4% combined ratio for the quarter included 4.5 points from net favorable prior year loss reserve development" and "less than 1 point in cat losses this year compared to 6 points in Q1 last year." (CFO Petrucelli)

  • "We produced a 21.1% expense ratio for the quarter compared to 20% last year" while "the other underwriting expense" ratio was "10.3%" vs. "10.5%" in Q1 2025; the expense ratio increase was attributed to "a higher net commission ratio resulting from higher reinsurance retentions." (CFO Petrucelli)

  • "We have noticed more aggressive competition in some long tail lines like construction over the last quarter," while small business property and multiple casualty divisions "experienced favorable underwriting conditions and strong growth." (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Stuart Winston)

Outlook

  • No formal EPS, revenue, or premium guidance was provided in this transcript; management described operating posture as "our continued focus on smaller transactions where margins continue to be robust" in a market where "E&S market conditions... continued to be competitive." (CEO Kehoe)

  • On profitability thresholds, management reiterated, "we always manage all of our product lines to a low 20s return on equity or greater" and added, "I wouldn't expect a meaningful deterioration" from the quarter’s "24%" ROE. (CEO Kehoe)

  • On reinsurance, management said it reviews retentions annually: "we'll look at it again this year, but I can't really commit at this moment to how the treaties will be placed" and noted "it's a 6/1 renewal date." (CEO Kehoe)

Financial results

  • "Diluted earnings per share... was $5.11 per share for the quarter compared to $3.71 per share" in Q1 2025. (CFO Petrucelli)

  • Investment and float were positioned as ongoing earnings levers: "net investment income increased by 26.5%" year-over-year in Q1, and "Kinsale's float... grew to $3.3 billion at March 31 from $3.1 billion at the end of 2025." (CFO Petrucelli)

  • Portfolio yield context remained similar: "Annual gross return was 4.5%" and "new money yields are averaging around 5%, with an average duration slightly above 4 years" on fixed maturities. (CFO Petrucelli)

Q&A

  • Daniel Cohen, BMO Capital Markets: Asked about quote/bind KPI volatility and whether to accept higher loss ratios for growth; Chairman of the Board, CEO & President Michael Kehoe said, "I wouldn't overthink" short-period movements and added, "we always manage all of our product lines to a low 20s return on equity or greater" and "I wouldn't expect a meaningful deterioration" from the quarter’s ROE.

  • Hristian Getsov, Wells Fargo: Pressed on whether accident-year loss ratio benefited from one-offs; Chief Actuary Salmaan Allibhai responded, "There's nothing out of the ordinary, no one-time adjustments" and cited seasonality where "the first quarter is a little bit higher." Getsov also asked about E&S homeowners down 22%; Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Stuart Winston said, "The high-value market is experiencing some increased competition" and "the average premium is dropping a little bit." Getsov asked about reinsurance amid a below-average hurricane forecast; CEO Kehoe said, "I can't really commit" on placement decisions ahead of the "6/1 renewal date."

  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: Asked about shifting casualty competition; Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Winston said competition from "fronts and MGAs and new companies" is increasing "specifically in construction" over "the last 4 to 5 months," while he added, "Anything related to auto, we're seeing meaningful opportunities there." On submissions, CEO Kehoe said ex-commercial property submissions were "up... 9%" and described the environment as "reasonably steady."

  • Pablo Singzon, JPMorgan: Asked about retention and whether tech-enabled competitors are pressing small business; Winston said, "New business hit ratios and renewal hit ratios have been consistent quarter-to-quarter for a long time." CEO Kehoe said on competitors’ tech efforts, "No," adding Kinsale can adopt AI faster because "we don't have legacy software."

  • Mark Hughes, Truist: Asked if property pricing is still drifting down; CEO Kehoe said, "We don't really have any good news to report there" and pointed to easier comps in the "second half of the year." Hughes asked about equity portfolio performance; CEO Kehoe said, "It's performing well" and noted it has "lagged the S&P" since late 2022 while matching the "Vanguard VYM." Hughes asked about cash from operations; CFO Petrucelli said cash flow could be viewed as "trending it with net written premiums."

  • Rowland Mayor, RBC: Asked for commercial property mix specifics; Winston clarified, "That commercial property division, that is the large shared and layered division." Kehoe said average premium in that division is "between $30,000 and $40,000 per policy" and added the prior average "might have been north of $50,000," citing a shift to smaller accounts.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on growth headwinds and sustainability questions, including "We don't really have any good news to report" on property competition and whether Kinsale would "deteriorate" loss ratios for growth. (CEO Kehoe; Daniel Cohen, BMO Capital Markets)

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience: "we continue to have a high level of confidence in our model" and "we remain very confident in the long-term strength and durability of the platform," while Q&A responses were more cautious on property and reinsurance specifics: "I can't really commit at this moment." (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Winston; CEO Kehoe)

  • Versus Q4 2025, management continued to frame competition as intense in large commercial property, but Q1 carried more explicit acknowledgment on property: "We don't really have any good news to report there," compared with prior-quarter stabilization language that "at some point... it should stabilize." (CEO Kehoe, Q1 2026; CEO Kehoe, Q4 2025)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1’s growth narrative leaned more heavily on mix shift to smaller policies ("$12,200 per policy") and added quarterly divisional disclosure, while Q4 emphasized capital return actions including "the $250 million buyback authorization" and a dividend increase to "$0.25" from "$0.17." (CEO Kehoe, Q1 2026; CEO Kehoe, Q4 2025)

  • Pricing commentary showed continued softening in the composite: Amwins index rate change of "a rate decrease of 3 1/3%" in Q1 vs. "a rate decrease of 2.7%" in Q4. (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Winston, Q1 2026; Winston, Q4 2025)

  • Profitability remained strong but the combined ratio moved from "71.7%" in Q4 to "77.4%" in Q1, with both quarters benefiting from "less than 1 point" of cat losses while Q1 referenced last year’s Q1 cat load of "6 points." (CFO Petrucelli, Q4 2025; CFO Petrucelli, Q1 2026)

Risks and concerns

  • Large commercial property remained the clearest growth and pricing pressure point: "an abundance of competition and falling rates" and "We don't really have any good news to report there." (CEO Kehoe)

  • Competitive intensity broadened in selected casualty areas: "more aggressive competition" in construction from "fronts and MGAs and new companies." (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Winston)

  • Management highlighted uncertainty around reinsurance structure ahead of renewal: "I can't really commit at this moment" on retention and treaty placement decisions. (CEO Kehoe)

Final takeaway

Kinsale management described Q1 2026 as a quarter where profitability remained strong alongside a deliberate shift toward smaller accounts, with growth constrained mainly by intensified competition and falling rates in large shared-and-layered commercial property. Executives reiterated a "low 20s" ROE operating target, pointed to continued momentum in submissions/quotes/binds outside commercial property, and emphasized that technology and AI adoption—enabled by an enterprise system without legacy software—remains central to efficiency and underwriting execution as market competition persists into the mid-year reinsurance renewal.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apogee forecasts fiscal 2027 sales of $1.38B-$1.43B and adjusted EPS of $2.70-$3.25 amid pricing and volume pressure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Apogee Enterprises (APOG) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • "I'm pleased to share that our results for the quarter were ahead of our expectations on both the top and bottom line despite what continued to be a dynamic and challenging environment." (CEO & Executive Chairman of the Board Donald Nolan)

  • "Performance Services successfully integrated UW Solutions into the segment. They delivered upon the first year financial targets for the acquisition of $100 million in revenue and adjusted EBITDA margin of at least 20%." (CEO & Executive Chairman of the Board Nolan)

  • "We actively managed our cost structure and manufacturing footprint to mitigate portions of direct and indirect tariffs while driving efficiencies across the organization." (CEO & Executive Chairman of the Board Nolan)

  • "Moving forward, we are enhancing these strategic pillars to position Apogee as a more growth-oriented customer-obsessed organization." (CEO & Executive Chairman of the Board Nolan)

  • "Net sales increased 1.6% to $351.4 million." (CFO & Executive VP Mark Augdahl)

Outlook

  • "For fiscal '27, we expect full year net sales between $1.38 billion and $1.43 billion and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $2.70 to $3.25." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

  • "We've set wider full year sales and EPS ranges to ensure our guidance reflects the realities of today's operating environment." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

  • "Looking ahead to the first quarter, we expect net sales to be slightly lower and adjusted EPS to be lower on a year-over-year basis." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

  • "Additionally, we expect interest expense of approximately $10 million and adjusted effective tax rate of 26% to 27% and capital expenditures between $35 million and $40 million." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

Financial results

  • Net sales were $351.4 million and adjusted diluted EPS was $0.92, with adjusted EBITDA margin of 12.1%. "Net cash provided by operating activities was $55.8 million in the quarter" and "in the fourth quarter, we repurchased $15 million of stock." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

  • Segment detail included Metals net sales of $110 million and Services backlog of $694 million, while Glass net sales were approximately $67 million. "Performance Surfaces net sales increased to over 13%, driven by volume growth supported by share gains in the retail and fine arts market channels." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

  • Full-year figures included "net sales increased 3.2% to $1.4 billion" and "net cash from operating activities was $122.5 million," while leverage was described as "a consolidated leverage ratio of 1.3x." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

Q&A

  • Julio Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked for aluminum assumptions and pricing offsets; CFO Augdahl: "I think we've seen about 87% increase in aluminum costs over the past year and 25% increases since -- just since January" and said the company is "implementing price as appropriate" including "surcharges or regular price."

  • Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked whether prior-year tariff impacts are mitigated and how new policy flows through; CFO Augdahl: "for F '26, we had about a $9 million impact on tariffs" and "it was a headwind in '26, it will be a tailwind now in '27."

  • Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked about embedded AI in manufacturing; CEO Nolan: "it's early days for us in AI for sure" and "we're rolling out Copilot across the company."

  • Gowshihan Sriharan, Singular Research, LLC: Asked if Metals is pruning low-margin work due to aluminum; CFO Augdahl: "we have not changed our product or customer mix" and CEO Nolan added, "at this price, aluminum is the best material for these applications."

  • Sriharan, Singular Research, LLC: Asked about Glass contract structures and input-price swings; CFO Augdahl: "the float suppliers do provide surcharges to us" and "we pass them on as well."

  • Sriharan, Singular Research, LLC: Asked what portion of SG&A efficiency is structural vs. incentive comp; CFO Augdahl: "we are reinstating our compensation programs" and "I do expect our overall SG&A rate to increase."

  • Sriharan, Singular Research, LLC: Asked whether UW/Performance Surfaces has capacity constraints; CEO Nolan: "we are investing in that plant" and "short term, we don't see a problem there."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative to skeptical, concentrating on cost inflation and durability of savings: "any effect baked in for the year-to-date rise in aluminum prices" (Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC) and "how much of that SG&A efficiency is truly structural versus temporarily depressed" (Sriharan, Singular Research, LLC).

  • Management slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, leaning on mitigation and acknowledging uncertainty: "we've set wider full year sales and EPS ranges" (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl) and "it's early days for us in AI" (CEO & Executive Chairman of the Board Nolan).

  • Versus the prior quarter, the discussion shifted from leadership transition to execution and inflation: "Stepping in as the interim CFO is our Chief Accounting Officer, Mark Augdahl" (CEO & Executive Chairman of the Board Nolan, prior call) compared with the current call’s focus on "elevated aluminum and fuel cost inflation" (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance framing moved from a tighter FY26 outlook to a wider FY27 range: "We now expect net sales to be approximately $1.39 billion and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $3.40 to $3.50" (CFO Mark Augdahl, prior call) versus "net sales between $1.38 billion and $1.43 billion" and "adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $2.70 to $3.25" (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl, current call).

  • Tariffs shifted from being quantified as a FY26 headwind to being positioned as a FY27 tailwind: "an updated estimate of the EPS impact from tariffs of approximately $0.30" (CFO Augdahl, prior call) versus "it was a headwind in '26, it will be a tailwind now in '27" (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl, current call).

  • Services momentum and backlog changed from "backlog for services ended the quarter at $775 million" (CFO Augdahl, prior call) to "Backlog for services ended the quarter at $694 million" (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl, current call).

Risks and concerns

  • "We anticipate continued competitive pricing and volume pressure in the Metals and Glass segments" and cited "elevated long-term interest rates" and "a dynamic macroeconomic environment." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

  • Aluminum inflation remained a central risk in Q&A: "very dynamic market" and "certainly a drag on the year" while the company works to "mitigate those impacts." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

  • Cost tailwinds were presented as time-bound in places: "tailwinds mostly impacting the first half" related to "prior year tariff costs that have since been mitigated." (CFO & Executive VP Augdahl)

Final takeaway

Management described Q4 results as ahead of expectations and emphasized cost actions and operating-system execution, while setting FY27 guidance at $1.38B-$1.43B of net sales and $2.70-$3.25 of adjusted EPS amid continued pricing and volume pressure in Metals and Glass, aluminum volatility, and the expected normalization of incentive compensation and health insurance costs, with tariff mitigation and Fortify-related savings positioned as offsets—especially early in the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Newmont surges on Q1 earnings topper, record free cash flow, $6B buyback
data.description:
Newmont (NEM) up 5.9% in Friday's trading after easily beating Q1 adjusted earnings estimates, as record gold prices helped offset lower ​production, and announcing plans to repurchase $6B in shares.

Q1 net income amounted to $3.3B, adjusted net income totaled $3.2B, or $2.90/share, adjusted EBITDA climbed to $5.2B, and free cash flow reached a quarterly record $3.1B.

Q1 attributable gold production fell to 1.3M oz from 1.54M oz in the year-earlier quarter, and gold all-in sustaining costs dropped to $1,029/oz, reflecting lower output at the Boddington mine ​in Australia due to bushfires, weaker grades and heavy rainfall at Tanami in Australia, ​and lower ⁠grades and planned maintenance at Lihir in Papua New Guinea and Cerro Negro in Argentina.

Newmont (NEM) maintained FY 2026 attributable gold production guidance at 5.26M oz, +/- 5%, gold AISC of $1,680/oz, as well as 9M oz of silver and 30K tons of copper, and attributable capital expenditure of $3.35B.

The company expects to generate 23% of total attributable production in Q2, slightly below Q1 levels, while warning Q2 costs would be "notably higher" than Q1 due to higher sustaining capital spending, lower silver production, and increased operating costs at mines including Boddington, Tanami, Lihir, and Peñasquito, as well as the full-quarter impact of Ghana's higher royalty and elevated oil prices.

"For every $10 per barrel ​change in oil prices, we would expect a ~$60M impact on costs, which equates to a ~$12/oz impact on all‑in sustaining costs," Newmont (NEM) interim CFO Peter Wexler ​said in the company's earnings conference call.

data.title: Eagle Financial highlights $275M loan pipeline as it monitors large problem relationship
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eagle Financial Services (EFSI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our first quarter results reflect continued progress executing against our long-term strategy even as we navigate a more normalized growth environment following the liquidity events that we discussed throughout 2025,” said Brandon Lorey (President, CEO & Director), while also noting that “earnings declined modestly from the fourth quarter,” alongside “margin expanded, the efficiency ratio improved meaningfully, and the credit quality remains well controlled.”

  • Lorey highlighted funding and balance sheet actions, saying “net interest margin increased to 3.63%, driven primarily by continued improvement in funding costs and the benefits of last year's balance sheet repositioning,” and added that “we reduced wholesale borrowings meaningfully during the quarter,” with “liquidity… ample” and capital “exceed[ing] well-capitalized regulatory thresholds.”

  • “We reported net income of $3.7 million compared to $4.3 million in the fourth quarter,” said Kathleen Chappell (Executive VP & CFO), adding “net interest income totaled $15.9 million” and “noninterest income totaled $4.9 million,” while “noninterest expense declined to $14.2 million, down $1.3 million from the fourth quarter.”

  • Joseph Zmitrovich (President & Chief Banking Officer of Bank of Clarke) framed Q1 lending as steady but impacted by runoff and sales: “Loan balances declined modestly during the quarter, driven primarily by the continued amortization of the Marine portfolio and the sale of approximately $7.5 million of SBA loan,” while also reporting “$81 million in loan closings in the first quarter” and a “solid” pipeline at “$275 million.”

Outlook

  • No formal EPS, revenue, or net interest margin guidance was provided in these prepared remarks.

  • Chappell flagged potential near-term volatility tied to a specific credit: “We are actively managing one large problem relationship that is well identified and closely monitored,” and “as we move into the second quarter, we expect to receive additional information that could result in either a resolution of the exposure or an incremental reserve build.”

  • On M&A positioning, Lorey said, “We continue to engage in conversations with potential bank partners that align with our community-focused model and long-term strategic objectives,” while emphasizing, “our approach to mergers and acquisitions remains disciplined and we will only pursue opportunities that clearly enhance the strength and value of our franchise.”

Financial Results

  • Chappell reported profitability of “net income of $3.7 million” and “$0.69 per diluted share,” with “return on average assets… 0.81%” and “return on average equity… 7.98%.”

  • Net interest margin was “3.63%,” and Chappell said the improvement was “driven largely by lower interest expense, particularly on deposits,” while “net interest income totaled $15.9 million… primarily due to lower average earning assets.”

  • Fee-related results reflected mix shifts: “wealth management fees declined sequentially following several elevated estate-related transactions in the fourth quarter,” which was “partially offset by higher gains on sales of loans, driven by increased SBA production with solid mortgage activity.”

  • Credit expense increased: “We recorded $2 million in provision for credit losses,” and Chappell added, “net charge-offs were negative for the quarter due to net recoveries,” while the provision “meaningfully strengthens our reserve position.”

  • Balance sheet changes included “total assets declined to $1.84 billion,” “total deposits declined slightly to $1.6 billion,” and “we fully paid down our remaining FHLB borrowing,” which Chappell said “materially reduced our reliance on wholesale funding and improved overall balance sheet flexibility.”

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts sentiment could not be assessed because no Q&A was included.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and balance sheet actions, including Lorey’s “the underlying performance of the franchise remains solid,” while also acknowledging a more cautious credit watch with Chappell’s “one large problem relationship.”

  • Versus the prior quarter’s prepared remarks, management language shifted from broader year-end momentum and “optimistic” framing toward more emphasis on normalized conditions and credit monitoring, while still reiterating disciplined M&A conversations.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Profitability declined from Q4 to Q1, with Q1 net income of $3.7 million and Q4 net income of $4.3 million, alongside Q1 EPS of $0.69 and Q4 EPS of $0.81.

  • Profitability drivers shifted toward efficiency gains and modest margin improvement in Q1: efficiency ratio was “68%” in Q1 versus “70.3%” in Q4, while net interest margin was “3.63%” in Q1 versus “3.61%” in Q4.

  • Credit discussion became more specific in Q1: Q4 emphasized stable NPAs at “0.77% of total assets,” while Q1 cited NPAs at “0.80% of total assets,” and introduced the possibility of “an incremental reserve build” tied to a “large problem relationship.”

  • Balance sheet and funding actions advanced in Q1, highlighted by Chappell’s “fully paid down our remaining FHLB borrowing,” while lending shifted from Q4 “$13.1 million in net loan growth” to Q1 “loan balances declined modestly,” affected by marine amortization, SBA sales, and several large payoffs.

Risks and Concerns

  • Chappell identified borrower-specific credit risk: “one large problem relationship” where upcoming information “could result in either a resolution of the exposure or an incremental reserve build,” with mitigation centered on monitoring and reserving, including “higher specific reserves related to 2 commercial and industrial relationships.”

  • Lorey cited a “competitive” operating environment, while emphasizing deposit pricing discipline: “Deposit pricing discipline and the runoff of our higher-cost funding continue to positively impact our spread.”

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as continued progress despite a “more normalized growth environment,” highlighting a higher net interest margin, improved efficiency, and the elimination of remaining FHLB borrowing, while flagging a monitored “large problem relationship” that could drive either resolution or additional reserves in Q2; loan balances were modestly lower due to marine runoff, SBA loan sales, and several payoffs, even as the company pointed to $81 million in closings and a $275 million pipeline and reiterated disciplined M&A engagement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bristol, Pfizer's Eliquis becoming available on Mark Cuban Cost Plus Drug
data.description:
• Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) and Pfizer's (PFE) blockbuster blood thinner Eliquis (apixaban) will launch on the Mark Cuban Cost Plus Drug website starting April 27.
  • A 30-day supply will cost $345. 
  • The price matches Bristol and Pfizer's existing Eliquis Direct-to-Patient program.
  • Eliquis is approved to reduce the risk of stroke and blood clots in individuals who have atrial fibrillation, as well as deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism.
